points.text: Enable rate limiting on password reset endpoint per IP and per account to curb abuse.
Add CAPTCHA (e.g., reCAPTCHA) to the reset form to block automated requests.
Implement exponential backoff and jitter on client retries to reduce thundering herd.
Introduce a short-lived queue (e.g., RabbitMQ) to decouple reset processing from HTTP response.
Cache recent reset tokens in Redis to avoid repeated DB writes for the same request.
Create a DB index on the email/username column used in reset lookups to speed queries.
Add read replicas for the user table and direct reset lookups to replicas.
Enable connection pooling and increase max connections to handle burst traffic.
Add index on password_reset_requests(user_id, request_time) to speed lookups.
Partition password_reset_requests by date to prune old data and improve query pruning.
Archive or purge old password reset entries regularly to keep table size manageable.
Use a read replica for reporting queries on reset data to offload the primary database.
Enable query caching for frequent token validation queries to reduce DB load.
Review and optimize token verification queries; ensure they use appropriate indexes.
Set isolation level to READ COMMITTED for reset inserts if consistency permits, to reduce locking.
Increase connection pool size and adjust max_connections to handle higher concurrent reset requests.
Deploy edge rate limiting (e.g., iptables or CDN) to cap password‑reset POSTs per IP per minute.
Add CAPTCHA or email‑verification step on the reset form to block automated abuse.
Enable HTTP 429 responses for excess requests; configure client‑side exponential backoff.
Scale the DB read replica pool and increase max_connections in PostgreSQL/MySQL to handle burst load.
Introduce a lightweight in‑memory cache (Redis) for recent reset tokens to reduce DB writes.
Tune Linux TCP backlog: sysctl net.core.somaxconn and net.ipv4.tcp_max_syn_backlog to accept more SYNs.
Activate SYN cookies (net.ipv4.tcp_syncookies=1) to mitigate SYN‑flood attacks during spikes.
Set socket SO_REUSEPORT on the service listeners to distribute connections across worker processes.
OUT_OF_SCOPE: The question pertains to security and performance engineering, which is outside the math_logic domain.
UNKNOWN: No applicable answer can be provided within the given scope.
Enable rate limiting on the password‑reset endpoint per IP and per account to stop rapid abuse.
Add CAPTCHA or challenge‑response to the reset form to block automated requests.
Implement IP reputation blocking for known abusive sources and temporary bans on repeated failures.
Queue password‑reset emails and token generation via a background worker to decouple from the web request.
Cache recent reset token requests in memory (e.g., Redis) to avoid duplicate DB lookups.
Scale the database temporarily: add read replicas or increase connection pool size to handle the spike.
Introduce exponential back‑off or jitter on client retries to reduce thundering‑herd effects.
Enable detailed logging and alerting for reset‑request volume thresholds to detect abuse early.
